1: magoi (magi, plural of magos) — originally a priestly caste of Persian Zoroastrians skilled in astronomy, interpretation of signs, and dream interpretation. By the 1st century the term had broadened to include wise men, court astrologers, and interpreters of omens throughout the eastern Mediterranean. Matthew does not specify three or kings — the church tradition of three kings derives from the three gifts (v.11). apo anatolon — from the east — the same direction from which the Babylonian exile came; now wise men from the nations seek the Jewish King.
11: prosekynesan auto — they worshiped/did obeisance to him — proskyneo (fall before in worship or homage) is used throughout Matthew for worship of Jesus (4:10, 8:2, 9:18, 14:33, 15:25, 28:9,17) and specifically for worship of God (4:10 quoting Deut 6:13). The Magi perform before the infant what the disciples perform before the risen Lord. edoran auto dora — they offered him gifts — the noun dora (gifts, offerings) carries cultic overtones; these are tribute-offerings appropriate to a king and priest.
2: ton astera autou en te anatole — his star in the rising/east — anatole can mean "east" (the direction) or "rising" (of a star: heliacal rising, the first appearance of a star above the eastern horizon at dawn, an astronomically significant event). The magi may have seen the star "in its rising" — at its first appearance. The definitive article (ton astera autou = his star) assumes prior knowledge of a messianic astral sign; Numbers 24:17 ("a star shall come out of Jacob") provides the OT background.
3: etarachthe — he was troubled, disturbed — aorist passive of tarasso (to agitate, throw into confusion; used in 14:26 of the disciples seeing Jesus on the water and in 27:65 for the disturbed watchers at the tomb). Herod's disturbance is political: a rival king of the Jews threatens his Idumean dynasty. Kai pasa Ierosolyma met' autou — and all Jerusalem with him — the city's collective anxiety parallels its rulers'; whether from genuine messianic concern or fear of Herod's violent reaction to perceived challenges is left ambiguous by Matthew.
4: archiereis kai grammateis tou laou — chief priests and scribes of the people — archiereis (chief priests, plural) encompasses the ruling priestly families, not merely the current high priest. Grammateis (Torah-scribes, learned interpreters) are distinguished from the Pharisees (not mentioned here) and function as the official biblical scholars. Their role is deeply ironic: they correctly identify Bethlehem (v.5) as the messianic birthplace from scripture but do not go to seek the child — biblical knowledge without response.
5: The chief priests and scribes cite Micah 5:2 without hesitation: en Bethleem tes Ioudaias — in Bethlehem of Judea — the specification "of Judea" distinguishes this Bethlehem from the Galilean Bethlehem (Josh 19:15). Their citation is accurate; their indifference is the irony. The learned authorities of Jerusalem know the scripture that the Magi (Gentiles from the east) had followed by astral observation. Matthew contrasts pagan seekers with Jewish experts: the Gentiles travel; the experts inform without moving.
6: The Micah citation (5:2) is modified significantly: oudamos elachiste ei en tois hegesin Iouda — by no means are you least among the rulers of Judah — changes the MT's "too small" to a positive declaration, and adds hostis poimanei ton laon mou ton Israel (who will shepherd my people Israel) from 2 Sam 5:2 (David's commission). The composite citation (Micah + 2 Samuel) is characteristic of Matthew's interpretive freedom in demonstrating scriptural coherence: the Messianic ruler is both Davidic birthplace (Micah) and Davidic shepherd-king (2 Sam).
7: ton chronon tou phainomenou asteros — the time of the appearing star — phainomai (present participle: the appearing, manifesting star) describes the star as currently visible/manifest. Herod determines the time akribos (exactly, precisely — the same adverb repeated in v.8 for his commission to the Magi). This precise timing becomes the basis for the massacre calculation in v.16: "two years old and under, according to the time he had determined." The detail is not incidental; it sets up the subsequent atrocity.
8: akribos exetasate — search carefully/precisely — the adverb akribos (exactly, precisely) repeats Herod's precision motif from v.7. The command to search carefully serves as the deceptive frame for his stated pious purpose (hopOs kagO elthon proskyneso auto — so that I too may come and worship him). Matthew presents the irony without editorial comment: the language of religious devotion (proskyneso = worship) is borrowed to cloak murder. The Magi are unwitting instruments of Herod's information-gathering.
9: kai idou ho aster — and behold, the star — the idou (behold!) signals the narrative's highlighting of a key event: the star's reappearance after their Jerusalem visit. Proegen autous — it went before them — the imperfect proegen (was going before) indicates ongoing movement of the star ahead of the Magi. Heos elthon estathē epano hou en to paidion — until having come it stood over where the child was — the aorist passive estathē (was stationed, halted) suggests a definitive stopping. The star-guidance echoes the wilderness pillar (Exod 13:21) in leading God's people to his purposes.
10: Echaresan charan megalen sphodra — they rejoiced a very great joy — the cognate construction (verb chairo + related noun chara) is a Hebraism (equivalent to the Hebrew infinitive absolute) used to intensify the expression: they rejoiced greatly. The adverb sphodra (very greatly, exceedingly) further intensifies. The construction appears in the LXX for superlative joy (e.g., 1 Kgs 1:40). The Magi's response to finding the star stationary is unbounded relief and celebration, marking the climactic resolution of their long journey.
12: Chresmatisthentes kat' onar — having been warned by a divine oracle in a dream — chrematizo in the passive (to receive a divine oracle, divine communication) is stronger than a general dream: the Magi receive a specific divine directive. The verb is used in LXX for divine responses (Jer 29:23; 37:2) and in NT for divine warnings (Heb 8:5; 11:7). Di' alles hodou anechoresan — through another road they withdrew — anachoreo (to withdraw, retire: used throughout Matt 2 for strategic repositioning) describes the Magi's obedient departure by an alternate route, thwarting Herod's intelligence-gathering.
13: Phyge eis Aigypton — flee into Egypt — pheugo (aorist imperative: flee!) conveys urgency. The command is delivered at night and obeyed at night (nyktos). Egypt as refuge is historically plausible (large Jewish population in Alexandria) and typologically resonant: the land of Israel's slavery becomes the Messiah's sanctuary. Heos an eipo soi — until I tell you — the conditional an with subjunctive (eipo = tell) makes the return contingent on a future divine communication, maintaining the pattern of Joseph's complete dependence on divine direction.
14: Anastas paralaben to paidion kai ten metera autou nyktos — having risen he took the child and his mother by night — the aorist participle anastas (having risen, stood up) is the same root as anastasis (resurrection), here simply "got up from sleep." The night departure mirrors the urgency of the Exodus (Exod 12:31-42: Israel departed from Egypt at night). Anechoreosen eis Aigypton — he withdrew into Egypt — the verb anachoreo appears again (vv.12, 13, 14, 22) as Matthew's term for divinely-directed strategic movement.
15: Ex Aigyptou ekalesa ton huion mou — out of Egypt I called my son — the citation of Hosea 11:1 in the MT (the LXX reads differently: ta tekna autou = his children). Matthew follows the MT reading, which has the singular "my son." Hosea 11:1 originally refers to Israel's exodus as God's son called out of Egypt. Matthew applies this typologically to Jesus: Israel as "son" collectively is the type; Jesus as "Son" individually is the antitype who recapitulates and surpasses Israel's history. The hina plerothe formula frames this as fulfillment of a deeper pattern, not merely verbal prediction.
16: Ethumothē lian — he was furious/enraged greatly — thymoo (to be enraged, infuriated) is a strong word for violent anger; the adverb lian (greatly, exceedingly) intensifies it. Anaireo — to do away with, kill — a strong term also used for executions (Acts 2:23; 7:52 of Jesus's death). Apo dietons kai katateron kata ton chronon hon ekribosen para ton magon — two years old and under, according to the time he had precisely determined from the Magi — the calculation explains the two-year range: Herod builds in a margin of error from the star's appearance time.
17: Tote eplerothe to rhēthen dia Ieremiou — then was fulfilled what was spoken through Jeremiah — note the absence of hina (in order that) that appears in other fulfillment formulas (1:22; 2:15, 23; 4:14). The adverb tote (then, at that time) replaces the purpose conjunction: Matthew does not say the massacre happened in order to fulfill, but that it was then fulfilled — a pastorally sensitive distinction that avoids making the children's deaths merely instrumental to prophetic fulfillment while still insisting on the scriptural correspondence.
18: The Jeremiah 31:15 citation: phone en Rama ekousthe, klauthmos kai odyrmos polys — a voice was heard in Ramah, weeping and great mourning — the aorist passive ekousthe (was heard) treats the prophetic text as a past event now seen in correspondence with a present event. Rachel weeping for her children (ta tekna autes) is a matriarchal mourning for descendants. The location of Ramah in Benjamin's territory (near Bethlehem in Judah's tribal area) was a deportation staging point for the Babylonian exiles (Jer 40:1). Matthew sees a structural correspondence between the Babylonian exile's weeping and Herod's massacre.
19: Teleutesantos de tou Herodou — after Herod died — the genitive absolute construction (teleuteo = to die, reach one's end) marks the temporal boundary of Jesus's Egyptian exile. Herod the Great died in 4 BCE, confirming that the birth of Jesus occurred before 4 BCE. Idou angelos kyriou phainetai kat' onar — behold, an angel of the Lord appears in a dream — the fourth occurrence of the divine-dream-announcement formula in Matthew 1-2 (1:20; 2:12, 13, 19), marking the sustained pattern of divine communication to Joseph throughout the infancy narrative.
20: Tethnekasin gar hoi zetountes ten psychen tou paidiou — for those who were seeking the child's soul/life are dead — the plural tethnekasin (perfect: have died and remain dead) and hoi zetountes (those who were seeking) are a precise verbal allusion to Exod 4:19 LXX where God tells Moses "for all those who were seeking your life are dead." Matthew's verbal echo is exact: Jesus's return from Egypt recapitulates Moses's return to Egypt after the death of those who sought his life, positioning Jesus as the new Moses from the very beginning of his life.
21: Anastas paralaben to paidion kai ten metera autou — having risen he took the child and his mother — the obedience formula repeats exactly (cf. v.14 departure formula), creating a structural parallel: departure (v.14) and return (v.21) framed by identical language. The anastas (having risen) + paralaben (took) sequence signals Joseph's immediate and complete compliance with the divine instruction, without deliberation or delay — the same pattern as v.24 (ch.1) where he took Mary as his wife after the angel's instruction.
22: Archelaus basileuei tes Ioudaias anti tou patros autou Herodou — Archelaus is reigning over Judea instead of his father Herod — Herod Archelaus (ruled 4 BCE–6 CE) was notorious for cruelty; he was deposed by Rome after Jewish complaints of his harsh rule and sent into exile. Phobētheis ekei apelthein — he was afraid to go there — the aorist passive phobētheis (having been made afraid, terrified) is a divine or providential fear, confirmed by a dream-warning. Joseph's fear is both rational (Archelaus's reputation) and divinely validated.
23: Nazōraios klethesetai — he will be called a Nazarene — the formula cites "the prophets" (plural, dia ton propheton) but no single OT text contains this phrase. Most likely explanations: (1) wordplay on Hebrew nezer (branch, shoot: Isa 11:1 — "a nezer from Jesse's roots") — the Nazarene/branch connection; (2) possible connection to Nazir (Nazirite, consecrated one: Num 6; Judg 13); (3) typological use of "prophets" as composite reference rather than single verbal citation. Matthew's formula signals prophetic fulfillment at the pattern level, not the specific-quotation level.
